hero.title: Praha
hero.sub:
Everything you need to know before you put your bag down.
Written for the person who just landed — and doesn't want to be charged the tourist price.

orient.title: Orient yourself in sixty seconds
orient.lede: Prague is small. The historic core is walkable end to end in about forty minutes. Almost everything you have heard of sits inside a bend of the Vltava river, split by the water into two halves.

map.caption: Not to scale. The river runs north; the castle is always on the west bank, uphill.

facts.def: Czech koruna (CZK / Kč). Not the euro. Anyone quoting you a price in euros is charging you for the privilege.
Czech. English is widely spoken in the centre, less so past the tram ring. Eight words will carry you a long way — see the phrases.
Clean, safe, free. Ask for kohoutková. Restaurants may still push bottled — you may decline.
112 from any phone, any language. Police 158 · Ambulance 155 · Fire 150.
Round up, or 10% for good service. Not obligatory. Tell the server the total you want to pay as you hand over money — don't leave coins on the table.
Walk ten minutes away from any famous thing before you eat, drink, or change money. That single habit will save you more than everything else on this page.

arrive.title: From the airport, without being robbed
arrive.lede: Václav Havel Airport sits 17 km west of the centre. There is no train and no metro line to the terminal. Here are your four options, honestly compared.

options.title: Trolleybus 59 + Metro A
options.body: Trolleybus 59 (it replaced the old bus 119 in 2024 — signs and old blog posts still say 119) runs to Nádraží Veleslavín, where you drop straight onto Metro line A.
options.note: Buy a 90-minute ticket, not a 30-minute one. The journey is longer than thirty minutes and inspectors do check.

options.title: Airport Express (AE)
options.body: Direct to Praha hlavní nádraží (the main railway station). Dedicated luggage space, no changes, no stairs.
options.note: Worth it with heavy bags or after a late flight.

options.title: Bolt or Uber
options.body: Book it in the app from inside the arrivals hall, then walk out to the marked ride-share pickup. Bolt is usually the cheaper of the two; Uber runs roughly 15–25% higher for the same trip.
options.note: Fine, fair, and metered. No negotiation, no surprises.

options.title: The man who approaches you
options.body: Drivers who walk up to you in the arrivals hall offering a taxi are not a service. They are a business model. The quote will be three to four times the real fare.
options.note: Never accept a ride from someone who approached you first. Anywhere in this city.

transit.title: Public transport, decoded
transit.lede: One ticket covers metro, tram, bus and funicular. It is timed, not per-journey — change lines as often as you like until it expires.

callouts.title: Get the app
callouts.body: It is called PID Lítačka. Digital tickets cost about 10% less than paper and 20% less than SMS, and there is nothing to validate.

callouts.title: Validate the paper one
callouts.body: A paper ticket is worthless until you stamp it in the yellow box the first time you board. Unstamped ticket = no ticket. The on-the-spot fine is 1,500 Kč, and inspectors work in plain clothes.

callouts.title: One exception
callouts.body: The 30- and 90-minute tickets are not valid on the Petřín funicular. A 24-hour ticket is.

money.title: Money, and the three ways they take it
money.lede: Prague's currency-exchange industry is one of the most sophisticated tourist traps in Europe. It is also completely avoidable, and the rules fit on a napkin.

rules.title: Never change money at a "0% commission" booth
rules.body: The commission is zero. The rate is the robbery. The gorgeous number in the window is what they'll give you if you buy euros from them — the rate for actually selling them your euros is in six-point type at the bottom. Clusters of these sit on Staroměstské náměstí and along Karlova, the street funnelling toward Charles Bridge. Walk past all of them.

rules.title: Use a bank's own ATM and take out koruna
rules.body: Look for Česká spořitelna, ČSOB or Komerční banka — the machines attached to actual bank branches. Standalone machines parked in tourist alleys exist to charge you for the convenience.

rules.title: When the card machine asks, always choose koruna
rules.body: "Would you like to be charged in euros / pounds / dollars instead?" is called dynamic currency conversion, and the answer is always no. Saying yes lets the merchant's payment terminal invent its own exchange rate. Always pay in CZK and let your own bank do the conversion.

money.foot: And never, under any circumstances, change money with a person on the street. That transaction has exactly one outcome.

converter.title: Quick sanity check
converter.lede: A rough feel for what a number on a menu actually means.
converter.tip: Rule of thumb: divide koruna by 25 for a close-enough euro figure. 100 Kč ≈ €4. A 500 Kč dinner ≈ €20.

sleep.title: Where to sleep
sleep.lede: We recommend neighbourhoods, not hotels. We have not slept in these buildings, and a guide that pretends otherwise is selling you something. Pick the right district and almost any decent room in it will serve you well.

hoods.name: Vinohrady
hoods.verdict: The best value in Prague, and it isn't close.
hoods.body: Leafy, residential, nineteenth-century apartment blocks, and the restaurants the city's own residents actually book. Roughly 20% cheaper than Old Town for the same standard of room, and five minutes on the metro from everything you came to see.

image.alt: A leafy boulevard in Vinohrady, Prague.
image.caption: Vinohrady — leafy, residential, and where the city actually eats.

hoods.name: Žižkov
hoods.verdict: For people who came for the pubs.
hoods.body: Hilly, scuffed, cobbled and unpretentious — historically the working-class quarter, now the highest density of drinking establishments in the city. Cheap beer, real neighbourhood, a bit of a climb home.

image.alt: A street in central Prague.
image.caption: Žižkov — hilly, scuffed and full of pubs. Photo: a Prague street, not Žižkov itself.

hoods.name: Malá Strana
hoods.body: Baroque streets under the castle, on the far side of Charles Bridge. It has its own sights, empties out beautifully in the evening once the day-trippers cross back, and remains a ten-minute walk from Old Town. You pay for that.

image.alt: Baroque facades in Malá Strana, Prague.
image.caption: Malá Strana — baroque streets under the castle.

hoods.name: Staré Město
hoods.body: You will be in the middle of it. You will also be in the middle of stag parties, pub crawls, and every restaurant in the city with a laminated nine-language menu. If you stay here, ask for a courtyard-facing room and mean it.

image.alt: An Old Town street in Prague.
image.caption: Staré Město — central, and loud until 3am.

sleep.tipsTitle: Three things worth knowing before you book

tips.title: Ask which way the room faces.
tips.body: Prague's historic buildings have paper-thin windows onto cobbled streets that amplify every voice. A courtyard room is a different holiday.

tips.title: Check the tram line, not the map distance.
tips.body: "1.5 km from Old Town Square" means nothing if it's uphill. "Four stops on the 22" means everything.

tips.title: The city charges a small nightly tourist fee
tips.body: per adult, often collected at the property and not shown in your online total. Budget for it; it is not a scam.

hotels.title: A few specific hotels
hotels.lede: Named hotels, not just districts — you asked. Same honesty rule as the restaurants: we have not slept in these. Prices are indicative and move hard with the season; guest scores are the aggregate on the big booking sites, not ours. Check current rates before you book.

items.name: EA Hotel Tosca
items.desc: A plain three-star on a quiet Vinohrady street, a few tram stops out. The budget end, in the value district.

image.alt: A leafy boulevard in Vinohrady, Prague.
image.caption: A Vinohrady street by it — representative, not the hotel.

items.name: Hotel Taurus
items.desc: A restored Art-Nouveau house on Vinohradská, on the tram line into the centre. Solid mid-range.

image.alt: The Hotel Taurus building on Vinohradská, Prague.

items.name: The Julius Prague
items.desc: A boutique aparthotel that guidebooks push hard, walkable to the best cafés. Upper-mid.

image.alt: An Old Town street in Prague.
image.caption: Near it in the centre — representative, not the hotel.

items.name: Le Palais Art Hotel
items.desc: A Belle-Époque palace turned five-star, a few quiet streets back from the noise. The splurge.

image.alt: The Le Palais Art Hotel building in Vinohrady, Prague.

items.name: Miss Sophie's
items.desc: A design hostel-hotel a few blocks off Wenceslas Square. Cheap, stylish, well-run — the backpacker-plus option.

image.alt: A street in central Prague near Wenceslas Square.
image.caption: A street near it in Nové Město — representative, not the hotel.

items.name: Mosaic House
items.desc: A big eco-minded design hotel with a hostel wing, near the river. Reliable mid-range with a bar in the basement.

image.alt: A leafy boulevard in central Prague.
image.caption: A nearby boulevard — representative, not the hotel.

items.name: Hotel Josef
items.desc: A glass-and-white design landmark tucked in the Old Town, minutes from everything. Boutique, minimalist, central.

image.alt: An Old Town street in Prague.
image.caption: An Old Town street by it — representative, not the hotel.

items.name: The Golden Well
items.desc: A Renaissance house built into the castle hill, with a terrace over the whole city. The romantic splurge.

image.alt: Baroque facades in Malá Strana, Prague.
image.caption: Malá Strana, where it sits — representative, not the hotel.

eat.title: Eat and drink like you live here
eat.lede: This is where the gap between the local price and the tourist price is widest — and where closing it takes the least effort. Usually about a three-minute walk.

compare.title: The same thing, two prices
compare.lede: Left: what it costs where Czechs buy it. Right: what it costs within sight of something famous.

rows.itemNote: a proper pub in Žižkov, Vinohrady or Holešovice

compare.foot: A beer that costs 100 Kč on Old Town Square costs 50 Kč three blocks away. You are not paying for the beer. You are paying for the view of people who also overpaid.

places.title: Four places to actually eat
places.lede: Why we name restaurants but not hotels: a bad meal costs you €10 and an evening, a bad hotel costs you €500 and a week. And a pub that has served the same street since 1895 is a verifiable fact in a way that "lovely rooms" is not. Every address below is checked against an official listing. We have still not eaten in any of them.

items.name: Lokál Dlouhááá
items.body: Nine Lokáls now exist; this is the first, and at just under seventy metres the longest. Twelve tanks of unpasteurised Pilsner Urquell feed the taps behind a steel bar. It is a chain, it is in the centre, and it is still the most reliable Czech kitchen within five minutes of Old Town Square.

rows.label: Where it sits
rows.value: above a local pub, below a trap

items.note: Published beer prices for this address range from 42 Kč to 100 Kč depending on the source and the year. Budget for the upper end.

image.alt: A half-litre of Czech lager on a wooden table in a pub.
image.caption: A Czech pub half-litre — not Lokál itself. Lokál pours Pilsner Urquell from twelve steel tanks.

items.more: Ask for the beer tankové — poured unpasteurised from the steel tanks, and the whole reason to come. At lunch the handwritten polední menu is the cheapest good meal in the centre. Walk in; no booking needed for two.

items.name: U Kroka
items.body: Family-run since 1895, at the foot of the Vyšehrad hill and a tram ride from every crowd in this guide. Home-style Czech cooking, done properly, in a room that fills with locals every single night. The city's own tourism board tells you to reserve. Reserve.

image.alt: Svíčková: beef in cream sauce with bread dumplings.
image.caption: Svíčková na smetaně — the dish, not this restaurant.

items.name: Havelská Koruna
items.body: A self-service canteen thirty seconds from Old Town Square, where a full plate of svíčková or goulash costs roughly what a single beer costs on the square outside. Take a tray, collect a paper ticket for everything you order, pay on the way out. No atmosphere whatsoever, and that is the point.

rows.label: The paper ticket
rows.value: hold onto it — you pay against it

image.alt: Beef goulash with bread dumplings, raw onion and a Pilsner.
image.caption: Guláš with knedlíky — the dish, not this canteen.

items.name: U Zlatého Tygra
items.body: Bohumil Hrabal drank here every afternoon for decades. It is still a working Czech pub, not a monument to one: the regulars' seats are reserved by unwritten right and are not surrendered to anybody. Arrive at three when it opens, take a genuinely empty chair, and say dobrý den to the table you join.

rows.label: Payment
rows.value: Cash only — and no sign says so

image.alt: A glass of Czech lager on a table.
image.caption: A Czech half-litre — not this pub. Nobody has photographed U Zlatého Tygra under a free licence.

items.name: Grand Café Orient
items.body: The world's only Cubist café, up a spiral stair on Ovocný trh. Coffee and cake in a 1912 interior nobody else thought to build.

image.alt: The Cubist Grand Café Orient building in Prague.
image.caption: The Grand Café Orient — this one really is the place.

items.name: Sisters Bistro
items.body: A tiny counter doing chlebíčky — the Czech open sandwich — done properly: cured meats, egg, potato salad, on good bread.

image.alt: Chlebíčky, Czech open-faced sandwiches.
image.caption: Chlebíčky — the dish, not this bistro.

items.name: U Fleků
items.body: A brewing house since 1499, touristy but real. Come for the vepřo-knedlo-zelo — roast pork, dumplings and kraut — and the dark lager.

image.alt: Roast pork with bread dumplings and sauerkraut.
image.caption: Vepřo-knedlo-zelo — the dish, not this pub.

items.name: Lahůdky deli counters
items.body: Not one place but a habit: the stand-up delis where locals grab a chlebíček and a coffee for pocket change between errands.

image.alt: Open-faced Czech deli sandwiches.
image.caption: Deli chlebíčky — the food, not a specific shop.

eat.trapTitle: How to spot the trap in four seconds
traps: A menu translated into six or more languages. Prices run about 20% higher the moment English appears prominently.
Someone standing outside inviting you in. Good pubs in this city have never needed to do that.
Photographs of the food on the menu.
Prices listed by weight — "100 g" — so the plate that arrives is 250 g and the bill is two and a half times what you read.
Anything already sitting on the table. Bread, nuts, olives. It is not complimentary. Ask, or push it away.

dishes.name: Svíčková na smetaně
dishes.desc: Beef in a root-vegetable cream sauce, bread dumplings, cranberry, a curl of whipped cream. The national dish, and better than that description sounds.

dishes.name: Guláš s knedlíky
dishes.desc: Thick beef goulash, dumplings, raw onion. Order it in a pub, never in a restaurant with a doorman.

dishes.name: Smažený sýr
dishes.desc: A deep-fried slab of cheese with tartar sauce. Culturally significant. Nutritionally indefensible.

dishes.name: Tank beer (tankové pivo)
dishes.desc: Unpasteurised lager pushed from a steel tank rather than a keg. If a pub advertises it, drink there. It is the single most reliable quality signal in Prague.

myth.title: About the trdelník
paras: The sugared chimney of dough sold from every second doorway, usually beneath a sign reading "OLD BOHEMIAN PASTRY", is not a traditional Czech pastry. It is not old, it is not Bohemian, and no Czech person has ever eaten one voluntarily.
It is perfectly nice. Eat it if you want. Just know that you are eating a marketing decision, and that the ice-cream-filled version costs roughly what a full lunch costs four streets away.

see.title: What to see, and what it really costs
see.lede: A surprising amount of the best of Prague is free. The paid tickets below are the ones worth the money — and we've marked exactly where the free version is just as good.

sights.name: Prague Castle
sights.body: The largest ancient castle complex in the world, and you can walk into most of it for nothing. All four courtyards, the front vestibule of St Vitus Cathedral, and the Royal Garden (April–October) cost zero. The changing of the guard at noon costs zero.
sights.note: Buy the circuit if you want inside the cathedral nave, the Old Royal Palace and Golden Lane. Otherwise walk the grounds free, and spend the 450 Kč on dinner.

image.alt: The second courtyard of Prague Castle, with the baroque Kohl Fountain.

sights.more: The circuit ticket is valid for two consecutive days, so you needn't rush all four sights into one visit. The courtyards are near-empty before 9am and after the tour buses leave around 5pm.

sights.name: Petřín Hill & Tower
sights.body: A wooded hill with the best panorama in the city on top of it. The park itself never closes and never charges. The lookout tower — a stubby cousin of the Eiffel — is the paid part, and the view from it beats the castle's.
sights.note: 299 steps. The lift is 150 Kč. Under-5s free. Remember: your 30- or 90-minute transit ticket does not cover the funicular up the hill — a 24-hour ticket does.

image.alt: The Petřín lookout tower rising from the wooded hill above Malá Strana.

sights.name: Vyšehrad
sights.body: The other castle. A tenth-century fortress on a cliff over the river, with ramparts you can walk, lawns you can lie on, and the national cemetery where Dvořák and Mucha are buried. Almost no crowds. Free, all year, always.
sights.note: If you have one spare afternoon and you are tired of being jostled, spend it here. This is the recommendation locals give each other.

image.alt: The view from the Vyšehrad ramparts, out over the Vltava.

sights.name: Charles Bridge
sights.body: Free at every hour, and worth a completely different amount depending which hour you pick. At two in the afternoon it is a slow-moving crush of selfie sticks, caricaturists and pickpockets.
sights.note: Set an alarm. Go at dawn. You will have a 650-year-old bridge, thirty baroque saints and the mist off the Vltava essentially to yourself, and you will understand why people write about this city.

image.alt: The Malá Strana bridgehead of Charles Bridge, late afternoon.

sights.more: Always open, always free. The statues you photograph are copies — the originals sit indoors in the Lapidárium, out of the weather. Cross before 7am to have it to yourself; by mid-morning it is shoulder to shoulder.

see.freeTitle: Free, and genuinely worth your time

free.name: The Astronomical Clock
free.desc: the hourly show is famously underwhelming; the clock itself is astonishing. Look at it, skip the crowd on the hour.

free.name: Letná Park beer garden
free.desc: the view over the river and its bridges, from above, with a cheap beer in hand.

free.name: Vrtba Garden & the Wallenstein Garden
free.desc: baroque terraces most visitors never find.

free.name: Náplavka
free.desc: the river embankment — Saturday farmers' market, and locals sitting with their legs over the water all summer.

free.name: Žižkov TV Tower
free.desc: from the outside, with the sculpted babies crawling up it. The observation deck costs; the sight of it does not.

free.name: Malá Strana after 21:00
free.desc: when the day crowds have gone and the gas lamps are lit.

freeBand.alt: Letná park in Prague — trees, paths and benches above the river.
freeBand.caption: Letná park, high above the Vltava — the free half of Prague is often the best half.

safe.title: Prague is very safe. Here is what to watch anyway.
safe.lede: Violent crime against visitors is rare — genuinely, statistically rare. What Prague has instead is an ecosystem of people who would like to overcharge you. Know the five, and none of them will work.

scams.title: The taxi that finds you
scams.body: Any driver who approaches you — at the airport, outside a club, on a square — is not looking for a fare. He is looking for someone who doesn't know the fare. Book Bolt, Uber, or Liftago in the app. If you must hail one, it must have a working meter and a printed price on the door.

scams.title: The exchange booth with a beautiful number
scams.body: Covered in section 03, and worth repeating because it costs people the most. The advertised rate is the direction that does not apply to you. There are no exceptions and there is no clever booth.

scams.title: "Would you like to pay in your own currency?"
scams.body: The card terminal is offering to convert for you at a rate it made up. Press the button for CZK, always, everywhere, including at the hotel.

scams.title: The plainclothes "police officer"
scams.body: Someone shows a badge, says there is a problem with counterfeit currency, and asks to inspect your wallet or passport. Real Czech police do not do this. Do not hand anything over. Say you will walk with them to the nearest police station — the encounter ends there.

scams.title: The crowd on the bridge and in tram 22
scams.body: Pickpocketing is the one crime you're realistically exposed to, and it happens exactly where you'd expect: Charles Bridge, Old Town Square, the astronomical clock on the hour, the metro at Můstek, and tram 22 to the castle. Front pocket. Bag zipped and in front of you. That's the whole defence.

speak.title: Twelve words that change how you're treated
speak.lede: Czechs do not expect you to speak Czech. They notice enormously when you try. Dobrý den on entering a shop and děkuji on leaving will measurably improve your week. Tap any phrase to copy it.

phrases.en: Hello — formal, and the correct greeting on entering any shop, pub or lift
Please — also "here you go", also "pardon?". Endlessly useful
The bill, please — say it to the server, they'll come to you
Tap water, please — it's safe, it's free
Cheers! — look them in the eye, clink, then tap the table before you drink

lines: Walk ten minutes away from anything famous before you eat, drink, or change money.
Say dobrý den when you walk in.
Cross Charles Bridge at six in the morning.
closing.sig: That's the whole guide. Enjoy Prague.

sources.note: Every price on this page was checked against a published source in July 2026. Prices change — Prague raised its transport fares on 1 January 2026, and will do so again. Treat these as the number you should expect, not a guarantee. If a price you're quoted is wildly above what's here, that's the point of this page.

links.label: What a beer actually costs in Prague
Prague City Tourism — U Zlatého tygra
